text:
One of the most prevalent hurdles to designing and training high performance deep learning (DL) models is data scarcity. While data parallelism is at the very core of many popular deep learning frameworks, and these frameworks are capable of high-accuracy, high-performance prediction, many fields are characterized by data scarcity. Fields with high-value predictive potential often correspond to areas where little to no data exists. For example, though medical imaging models show promising accuracy at classifying cancer, much of the available training data is sourced from healthy individuals due to stigma, rarity, or the high cost associated with collecting data from actual cancer patients. Collecting the relevant data is also usually a very expensive and time-consuming process. The process of labeling the training data not only entails significant human resources but also requires a deep understanding of the underlying physics of the phenomena described by the training data and is often highly subjective and open to interpretation.

Learn good representations (features) means for a Deep Learning (DL) model to learn these representations robustly it needs to see a lot of training data. Thus most of the times more data is better and larger datasets give us a more accurate and reliable model. Thus this factor is a primary reason why many domains have not yet adopted DL technology. For building an AI based reliable application one always needs a lot of good data.

Training Deep Learning models is a challenging task that involves several issues that have been studied extensively in the literature. This paper provides a survey of the most relevant and state-of-the-art solutions proposed for training such models. We start by providing a taxonomy of learning strategies and modern Deep Learning (DL) architectures. Then, we move to the most advanced solutions to deal with some of the most critical scenarios for the training of a DL model, such as scarcity of training data, class imbalance, and lack of generalization. These scenarios include Transfer Learning (TL), Self-Supervised Learning (SSL), Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), specialized Model Architectures (MA), Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINN), and the Deep Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (DeepSMOTE).

Beyond the usual recipes of techniques to overcome the scarcity of data, we provide several tips for data acquisition and recommendations for the use of robust data sets for training. Finally, we focus on several fields where data scarcity is a constant obstacle, including Electromagnetic Imaging (EMI), Civil Structural Health Monitoring, Medical Imaging, Meteorology, Wireless Communications, Fluid Mechanics, Microelectromechanical Systems (MEMS), and Cybersecurity, suggesting possible ways to generate synthetic or augmented data for each of these fields. To the best of our knowledge, this survey is the first to focus on the many aspects of deep learning related to data scarcity and possible ways to alleviate them.
explanation: The passage uses highly generic, formulaic language typical of AI-generated summaries (broad claims, standardized acronyms, and broad topic coverage) and lacks concrete specifics or citations. While a human-authored survey could resemble this, the consistency and breadth without unique insights increase the likelihood of AI authorship.
